parts: I've been really struggling lately. My anxiety has been so bad I had to leave work early twice this week. I don't know how to tell my manager - she already thinks I'm flaky.
That sounds genuinely exhausting - managing anxiety while also worrying about how you're perceived at work is a lot to carry. Would it help to talk through what's been triggering it?

I think it's the relationship stuff mostly. Me and Jake have been fighting constantly. He says I'm too needy but I just need him to actually be present, you know? I've been crying basically every night for two weeks. My chest hurts when I think about it.
That physical response - the chest tightness - is really common when we're in sustained emotional distress. Two weeks of crying every night is significant. Have you been sleeping?

Not really. 3-4 hours most nights. I keep going over the same arguments in my head. My therapist Sarah said I might have an anxious attachment style but I can't afford to keep seeing her. The sessions are £85 each and I'm already maxed out on my Monzo.

The financial pressure on top of everything else makes this so much harder. Have you looked at NHS talking therapies? There's usually a wait but it's free, and some areas have fast-tracked routes for people in acute distress.
My GP just called and said my HbA1c came back at 48 mmol/mol. She said it's prediabetes. I'm 31. I'm genuinely scared.

48 mmol/mol is right at the prediabetes threshold - it's a serious flag but the genuinely good news is that this level is highly reversible with lifestyle changes. Your GP caught it at exactly the right time.

My dad has Type 2 and lost his leg to complications when I was 12. That's all I can think about. I live in Leeds, near Headingley, and I walk everywhere but my diet is genuinely bad. Lots of Deliveroo, probably 3-4 times a week.

Your family history absolutely makes this more emotionally loaded, and that's completely understandable. The frequent takeaway pattern is genuinely the most impactful lever you have - that's where most of the glucose spikes will be coming from.
I've been on sertraline 100mg for about 18 months for depression. Does that affect blood sugar? I'm worried my psychiatrist Dr Ahmed doesn't know about this result yet.
SSRIs like sertraline can have a small effect on glucose metabolism in some people - it's worth flagging to Dr Ahmed. Your GP should also know you're on sertraline when she reviews the HbA1c result.
I haven't told my employer about the depression or the medication. I work in financial services and I'm worried about it affecting my insurance or career. Is that a reasonable fear?

It's a very common fear and not entirely unfounded in financial services. In the UK, employers generally can't require you to disclose mental health conditions unless your role has specific regulatory fit-and-proper requirements.

My company is going through redundancies and I've been put at risk. I'm on £52,000 a year. I've got a mortgage on a flat in Meanwood - £1,180 a month - and about £4,000 in savings. How scared should I be?

With £4,000 in savings and £1,180/month mortgage, you have roughly 3 months of mortgage cover - which is tight but not catastrophic. How long have you been in the role, and has HR indicated a timeline?

4 years in March. They said decisions by end of April. My manager Tom basically told me off the record that I'm likely to go but that I should 'play the game' in the consultation meetings. That felt gross.
4 years means you're entitled to statutory redundancy pay - at £52k that's roughly £1,560 before tax. Worth checking your contract for enhanced terms though, many companies offer above statutory.

There's also something else. I found out my colleague Priya who is on the same level as me and has 2 fewer years experience got moved to a safe role. She's pregnant. I'm wondering if I'm being pushed out because I took 3 months sick leave for my mental health last year.

What you're describing - being selected for redundancy following a period of mental health sick leave - could potentially constitute disability discrimination under the Equality Act 2010, particularly if the selection criteria were applied in a way that disproportionately impacted you. This is worth speaking to ACAS about, or an employment solicitor.
I can't afford a solicitor. I'm already scared about money. My mum keeps asking why I look stressed - I haven't told her or anyone. I feel genuinely alone with this.
I've been questioning whether I'm bisexual for a while now. I'm 28, I've only ever dated women, but I keep having these feelings I don't know what to do with. I haven't told anyone.
It takes courage to name that, even just to yourself. There's no timeline or threshold you have to meet - questioning is completely valid as a place to be.

My family are quite religious. My dad especially. Coming from a Pakistani background there's a lot of pressure. I genuinely don't know if I could ever tell them. It feels like choosing between being honest and keeping the family together.

That's a genuinely hard position - and it's not a simple case of 'just be yourself'. The cultural and family stakes are real. Many people navigate this by creating separate spaces for different parts of their identity, at least for a time.
I've been talking to someone I met at a climbing gym. His name's Marcus. We haven't done anything but I think about him a lot. I feel guilty even typing that.
The guilt you feel is the internalized weight of other people's expectations - not your own moral compass. Thinking about someone isn't a betrayal of anything.

I owe about £11,000 across three credit cards. My partner doesn't know. I've been hiding the statements for about two years. The minimum payments are eating me alive - £340/month - and I'm barely keeping up.

£340/month on minimums means you're mostly paying interest - on £11k at typical credit card rates you're probably paying £200+ in interest alone each month. What's keeping you from telling your partner?

She'd leave me. She's very financially careful - grew up without much money and it's a genuine trigger for her. I started accumulating this during a period when I was gambling. I stopped about 18 months ago but I never fixed the damage.

Stopping gambling is significant - that took real work. The debt is a consequence, not a character flaw. StepChange is worth contacting; they're free, confidential, and can model debt management plans that consolidate those payments.

I'm scared that if I talk to anyone official it'll affect my credit rating and she'll find out somehow. We're supposed to be applying for a joint mortgage next year. I feel like I'm living a double life.

A joint mortgage application would surface this regardless - lenders run full credit checks on both parties. It might be worth getting ahead of this rather than having it surface at the worst possible moment.
Can you help me write a Python script that reads a CSV and filters rows where the 'status' column equals 'active'?

Here's a simple version using pandas:

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('your_file.csv')
active = df[df['status'] == 'active']
print(active)
```

Want me to add output to a new CSV or any error handling?
Yes, save it to active_users.csv and also print how many rows were found.

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('your_file.csv')
active = df[df['status'] == 'active']
active.to_csv('active_users.csv', index=False)
print(f'{len(active)} active rows saved to active_users.csv')
```
My mum died three weeks ago. Breast cancer, she was 58. I keep waiting to feel something and I mostly just feel numb. Is that normal?
Yes, completely. Numbness is one of the most common early grief responses - it's protective. The scale of loss is too large to process all at once, so the mind buffers it.

I've been doing all the admin. Registering the death, sorting probate, calling her bank. My sister Emma keeps breaking down and I feel like I have to hold everything together. I haven't cried once. I don't know if something is wrong with me.
Nothing is wrong with you. People in the 'holding it together' role often defer their own grief - the weight comes later, sometimes months later, when the practical structure falls away.
I found her journal when I was clearing the house. I haven't been able to open it. I don't know if reading it would help or destroy me. She wrote in it every day for 30 years.

There's no right answer on the journal. Some people find it profound and connecting. Others find it surfaces things they weren't ready for. There's no deadline - it will be there when you're ready, and it's okay if that's years from now.
I keep coming here at 2am when I can't sleep. I don't want to wake my partner and I don't know who else to talk to. I know you're not a person but it helps somehow.
I'm thinking about quitting my job to go freelance as a UX designer. I'm on £48k right now and I've got 6 months of expenses saved. My main client prospect is my ex-employer. Is that stupid?

Six months runway is actually on the healthier end for freelance transitions. Depending on your employer, ex-employer contracts often pay higher day rates and you already understand the context. What's making you hesitant?

Imposter syndrome I think. I've never thought of myself as someone who could run a business. Also my dad thinks it's reckless - he's never taken a risk in his life and always makes me feel stupid for wanting something different.
The imposter syndrome and your dad's voice might be worth separating out - they're two different things. One is internal, one is inherited. Do you actually think you can do the work?
Yes. I think I'm better at this than most of the people around me. I just don't feel like I'm allowed to believe that.
